title: Katze und Maus in Gesellschaft
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 2. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly, including daß, Rath, nöthig, Wirthschaft, gieng/fieng, that/thun, Base, Gevatter and Pathen.
about: The second tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 2), a wry animal fable. A cat talks a mouse into keeping house together and laying up a pot of fat for winter, hidden for safety under the church altar; three times the cat slips away — each time on the pretext of standing godmother at a christening — to lick secretly at the pot, and names the imaginary godchildren Hautab (‘skin off’), Halbaus (‘half gone’) and Ganzaus (‘all gone’) after how far she has emptied it. When winter comes and the theft comes to light, the cat swallows the mouse as well. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, Rath, nöthig, gieng and Gevatter.

sentences.t: Eine Katze hatte Bekanntschaft mit einer Maus gemacht und ihr so viel von der großen Liebe und Freundschaft vorgesagt, die sie zu ihr trüge, daß die Maus endlich einwilligte mit ihr zusammen in einem Hause zu wohnen und gemeinschaftliche Wirthschaft zu führen.
sentences.l: A cat had made acquaintance with a mouse and told her so much of the great love and friendship that she bore toward her, that the mouse at last consented to live together with her in one house and to keep a common household.
sentences.i: A cat had struck up an acquaintance with a mouse, and talked to her so much of the great love and friendship she felt for her, that the mouse at last agreed to live together with her in one house and keep house in common.
sentences.n: „Bekanntschaft machen“ = to make someone’s acquaintance. „die sie zu ihr trüge“ — trüge is subjunctive II of tragen for reported feeling (the love she *said* she bore her); „Liebe zu jemandem tragen“ is an elevated ‘to bear/feel love for someone.’ Wirthschaft carries the old -th- (modern Wirtschaft); „gemeinschaftliche Wirthschaft führen“ = to run a joint household.

sentences.t: „Aber für den Winter müssen wir Vorsorge tragen, sonst leiden wir Hunger,“ sagte die Katze, „du Mäuschen, kannst dich nicht überall hinwagen und geräthst mir am Ende in eine Falle.“
sentences.l: „But for the winter we must make provision, else we suffer hunger,“ said the cat, „you, little-mouse, cannot venture yourself everywhere and will get me in the end into a trap.“
sentences.i: “But we must lay something by for the winter, or we shall go hungry,” said the cat; “you, little mouse, cannot go venturing about everywhere, and will end up walking into a trap.”
sentences.n: „Vorsorge tragen“ = to make provision, take precautions. Mäuschen is a coaxing diminutive of Maus (‘little mouse’) — Grimm’s tales are full of these -chen/-lein forms. geräthst is the 1857 spelling of gerätst (geraten, ‘to get/fall into’); the dative „mir“ is a dative of interest — ‘get yourself, on me, into a trap.’

sentences.t: Der gute Rath ward also befolgt und ein Töpfchen mit Fett angekauft.
sentences.l: The good counsel was thus followed, and a little-pot with fat was bought.
sentences.i: So the good advice was heeded, and a little pot of fat was bought.
sentences.n: Rath is the 1857 spelling of Rat (‘counsel, advice’), with the old -th-. ward is the archaic preterite of werden, here forming the passive ‘was followed’; you meet ward throughout Grimm. The single ward carries over to the second clause: „[ward] ein Töpfchen … angekauft.“

sentences.t: Sie wußten aber nicht wo sie es hinstellen sollten, endlich nach langer Überlegung sprach die Katze „ich weiß keinen Ort, wo es besser aufgehoben wäre, als die Kirche, da getraut sich Niemand etwas wegzunehmen: wir stellen es unter den Altar und rühren es nicht eher an als bis wir es nöthig haben.“
sentences.l: But they did not know where they should put it; at last, after long reflection, the cat said, „I know no place where it would be better kept than the church: there no one dares to take anything away; we put it under the altar and do not touch it before we have need of it.“
sentences.i: But they did not know where to put it; and at last, after long consideration, the cat said, “I know no better place to keep it than the church — there no one dares to make off with anything; we’ll put it under the altar, and not touch it until we truly need it.”
sentences.n: „aufgehoben wäre“ — aufheben here = to keep, store; the subjunctive wäre marks a hypothetical (‘where it *would be* better kept’). „sich getrauen“ = to dare (reflexive). „rühren … an“ is the separable verb anrühren (‘to touch’), split around the clause; nöthig (modern nötig) keeps the 1857 -th-.

sentences.t: Das Töpfchen ward also in Sicherheit gebracht, aber es dauerte nicht lange, so trug die Katze Gelüsten danach und sprach zur Maus „was ich dir sagen wollte, Mäuschen, ich bin von meiner Base zu Gevatter gebeten: sie hat ein Söhnchen zur Welt gebracht, weiß mit braunen Flecken, das soll ich über die Taufe halten.
sentences.l: The little-pot was thus brought into safety, but it did not last long, so the cat felt a craving for it and said to the mouse, „what I wanted to tell you, little-mouse: I have been asked by my cousin to be godparent; she has brought a little-son into the world, white with brown spots, which I am to hold over the baptism.
sentences.i: So the little pot was brought to safety; but before long the cat felt a craving for it, and said to the mouse, “What I meant to tell you, little mouse — my cousin has asked me to stand godmother: she has brought a little son into the world, white with brown spots, and I am to hold him at the font.
sentences.n: „zu Gevatter bitten / gebeten“ = to ask someone to be a godparent (Gevatter is the old word for godfather/sponsor); „über die Taufe halten“ = to hold the child at the font, i.e. act as godparent. Base is an archaic word for a female cousin or kinswoman. This whole errand is the cat’s lie — her cover for sneaking off to the pot of fat.

sentences.t: Laß mich heute ausgehen und besorge du das Haus allein.“
sentences.l: Let me go out today and see to the house alone.“
sentences.i: Let me go out today, and you look after the house by yourself.”
sentences.n: „Laß mich … ausgehen“ — laß is the old imperative of lassen (‘let’); ausgehen (to go out) is separable. „besorge du“ places the pronoun du after the imperative for emphasis: ‘*you* see to the house.’

sentences.t: „Ja, ja,“ antwortete die Maus, „geh in Gottes Namen, wenn du was Gutes ißest, so denk an mich: von dem süßen rothen Kindbetterwein tränk ich auch gerne ein Tröpfchen.“
sentences.l: „Yes, yes,“ answered the mouse, „go in God’s name; if you eat something good, then think of me: of the sweet red childbed-wine I too would gladly drink a little-drop.“
sentences.i: “Yes, yes,” answered the mouse, “go, in God’s name; and if you eat something good, think of me — I too would gladly sip a drop of the sweet red childbed wine.”
sentences.n: „in Gottes Namen“ = ‘in God’s name,’ a blessing on the errand. ißest is the 1857 form of isst. Kindbetterwein is the wine given to a woman in childbed (Kindbett); rothen keeps the old -th- (modern roten). tränk is a shortened subjunctive II of trinken (‘I would drink’).

sentences.t: Es war aber alles nicht wahr, die Katze hatte keine Base, und war nicht zu Gevatter gebeten.
sentences.l: But it was all not true: the cat had no cousin and had not been asked to be godparent.
sentences.i: But none of it was true: the cat had no cousin, and had not been asked to stand godmother at all.
sentences.n: The narrator steps in to expose the lie outright. „zu Gevatter gebeten“ repeats the idiom from before — the fictitious christening is pure pretext.

sentences.t: Sie gieng geradeswegs nach der Kirche, schlich zu dem Fetttöpfchen, fieng an zu lecken und leckte die fette Haut ab.
sentences.l: She went straightway to the church, crept to the little-fat-pot, began to lick, and licked the fatty skin off.
sentences.i: She went straight to the church, crept up to the little pot of fat, began to lick, and licked off the greasy top.
sentences.n: gieng and fieng are the 1857 -ie- spellings of ging and fing. „leckte … ab“ is the separable verb ablecken (‘to lick off’). „die fette Haut“ — the skin of fat congealed on top. Note Haut (‘skin’) and ab (‘off’): the cat’s first stolen name, „Hautab,“ is about to come from exactly these words.

sentences.t: Dann machte sie einen Spatziergang auf den Dächern der Stadt, besah sich die Gelegenheit, streckte sich hernach in der Sonne aus und wischte sich den Bart so oft sie an das Fetttöpfchen dachte.
sentences.l: Then she made a walk on the roofs of the city, looked over the lie of things, stretched herself afterward out in the sun, and wiped her whiskers as often as she thought of the little-fat-pot.
sentences.i: Then she took a stroll over the rooftops of the town, surveyed the prospect, afterward stretched herself out in the sun, and wiped her whiskers every time she thought of the little pot of fat.
sentences.n: Spatziergang is the 1857 spelling of Spaziergang (‘a walk, stroll’). „besah sich die Gelegenheit“ — here Gelegenheit means the lie of the land, ‘the situation, the prospect.’ „streckte sich … aus“ is the separable reflexive sich ausstrecken (‘to stretch out’). „den Bart wischen“ — of a cat, to wipe its whiskers, here in smug contentment.

sentences.t: Erst als es Abend war, kam sie wieder nach Haus.
sentences.l: Only when it was evening did she come home again.
sentences.i: Not until evening did she come home again.
sentences.n: „Erst als …“ = ‘not until …’; after the fronted time-clause the verb comes before the subject (‘came she’).

sentences.t: „Nun, da bist du ja wieder,“ sagte die Maus, „du hast gewiß einen lustigen Tag gehabt.“
sentences.l: „Well, there you are again,“ said the mouse, „you have surely had a merry day.“
sentences.i: “Well, here you are again,” said the mouse; “you must have had a merry day of it.”
sentences.n: gewiß (1857 for gewiss) = ‘surely, certainly.’ „da bist du ja wieder“ — the particle ja adds warmth, ‘why, there you are.’

sentences.t: „Es gieng wohl an“ antwortete die Katze.
sentences.l: „It went well enough,“ answered the cat.
sentences.i: “It was passable enough,” answered the cat.
sentences.n: „Es gieng wohl an“ — the separable angehen here in the sense ‘to be tolerable, pass muster’; a studiedly noncommittal answer. gieng is again the old spelling of ging.

sentences.t: „Was hat denn das Kind für einen Namen bekommen?“ fragte die Maus.
sentences.l: „What name, then, did the child get?“ asked the mouse.
sentences.i: “And what name did the child get?” asked the mouse.
sentences.n: „denn“ is a conversational particle giving the question a curious, ‘so tell me’ tone. „was für ein …“ is a frame meaning ‘what kind of …,’ here simply ‘what name.’

sentences.t: „Hautab“ sagte die Katze ganz trocken.
sentences.l: „Skin-off,“ said the cat quite drily.
sentences.i: “Skin-off,” said the cat, dead-pan.
sentences.n: Here the running joke begins. „Hautab“ is the cat’s invented christening-name, made from „Haut ab“ (‘skin off’) — exactly what she did to the pot of fat in licking off the top. She will invent two more such names (Halbaus, Ganzaus), each secretly reporting how far she has emptied the pot. „ganz trocken“ = perfectly drily, deadpan.

sentences.t: „Hautab,“ rief die Maus, „das ist ja ein wunderlicher und seltsamer Name, ist der in eurer Familie gebräuchlich?“
sentences.l: „Skin-off,“ cried the mouse, „that is indeed a wondrous and strange name; is it customary in your family?“
sentences.i: “Skin-off!” cried the mouse. “Why, what an odd and outlandish name — is that a usual one in your family?”
sentences.n: gebräuchlich = customary, in common use. The mouse, hearing only nonsense, misses entirely that the ‘name’ describes a theft.

sentences.t: „Was ist da weiter,“ sagte die Katze, „er ist nicht schlechter als Bröseldieb, wie deine Pathen heißen.“
sentences.l: „What is there further,“ said the cat, „it is no worse than Crumb-thief, as your godchildren are called.“
sentences.i: “What of it?” said the cat. “It’s no worse than Crumb-thief, which is what your godchildren are called.”
sentences.n: „Was ist da weiter“ = ‘what of it, what’s wrong with that.’ Bröseldieb (‘Crumb-thief,’ from Brösel ‘crumb’ + Dieb ‘thief’) is the cat’s tart invention for a mouse’s child — a jab that also, unknowingly, names the mouse’s own kind for what they are. Pathen is the 1857 spelling of Paten (‘godchildren’).

sentences.t: Nicht lange danach überkam die Katze wieder ein Gelüsten.
sentences.l: Not long thereafter a craving came over the cat again.
sentences.i: Not long afterward a craving came over the cat once more.
sentences.n: „überkam“ — überkommen (‘to come over, seize’) governs die Katze as its object: a longing overtook her. This opens the second of the tale’s three parallel episodes.

sentences.t: Sie sprach zur Maus „du mußt mir den Gefallen thun und nochmals das Hauswesen allein besorgen, ich bin zum zweitenmal zu Gevatter gebeten, und da das Kind einen weißen Ring um den Hals hat, so kann ichs nicht absagen.“
sentences.l: She said to the mouse, „you must do me the favour and once again see to the household alone; I am asked to be godparent for the second time, and since the child has a white ring around its neck, so I cannot refuse it.“
sentences.i: She said to the mouse, “You must do me the favour of looking after the household on your own again; I’ve been asked to stand godmother a second time, and since the child has a white ring round its neck, I can’t say no.”
sentences.n: thun is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’). „zum zweitenmal“ = for the second time. absagen (‘to decline, cry off’) is separable; „ichs“ = ich es. The white ring is a fresh embroidery on the same lie.

sentences.t: Die gute Maus willigte ein, die Katze aber schlich hinter der Stadtmauer zu der Kirche und fraß den Fetttopf halb aus.
sentences.l: The good mouse consented; the cat, however, crept behind the city-wall to the church and ate the fat-pot half out.
sentences.i: The good mouse consented; but the cat crept along behind the city wall to the church, and ate the pot of fat half empty.
sentences.n: „willigte ein“ is the separable verb einwilligen (‘to consent’). „fraß … aus“ — fressen is the verb for an animal’s eating (greedy, devouring); ausfressen = to eat out, empty. „halb aus“ (‘half out’) is the raw material of the next invented name, Halbaus.

sentences.t: „Es schmeckt nichts besser,“ sagte sie, „als was man selber ißt,“ und war mit ihrem Tagewerk ganz zufrieden.
sentences.l: „Nothing tastes better,“ she said, „than what one eats oneself,“ and was quite content with her day’s-work.
sentences.i: “Nothing tastes better,” she said, “than what one eats oneself,” and she was thoroughly pleased with her day’s work.
sentences.n: A cynical proverb turned to her own theft. „was man selber ißt“ — selber = ‘oneself.’ Tagewerk = a day’s work, one’s day’s labour — here her ‘labour’ was the plundering.

sentences.t: Als sie heimkam, fragte die Maus „wie ist denn dieses Kind getauft worden?“
sentences.l: When she came home, the mouse asked, „how was this child christened, then?“
sentences.i: When she came home, the mouse asked, “And what was this child christened?”
sentences.n: heimkam is the separable heimkommen (‘to come home’). „getauft worden“ = ‘been baptized’ (passive of taufen). denn again lends the question its inquisitive tone.

sentences.t: „Halbaus“ antwortete die Katze.
sentences.l: „Half-gone,“ answered the cat.
sentences.i: “Half-gone,” answered the cat.
sentences.n: The second invented name: „Halbaus,“ from „halb aus“ (‘half out/gone’) — her own report that the pot is now half empty, once more heard by the mouse as mere nonsense.

sentences.t: „Halbaus! was du sagst! den Namen habe ich mein Lebtag noch nicht gehört, ich wette der steht nicht in dem Kalender.“
sentences.l: „Half-gone! what you say! that name I have not heard in all my life; I wager it does not stand in the calendar.“
sentences.i: “Half-gone! The things you say! I’ve never in my life heard such a name — I’ll wager it’s not in the calendar.”
sentences.n: „mein Lebtag“ = ‘in all my life’ (an old set phrase). „ich wette“ = I bet, I wager. The Kalender (almanac) listed saints’ name-days — a name ‘not in the calendar’ is no proper Christian name at all. „der steht nicht“ — the demonstrative der points back to Namen.

sentences.t: Der Katze wässerte das Maul bald wieder nach dem Leckerwerk.
sentences.l: The cat’s mouth soon watered again after the dainty-work.
sentences.i: Before long the cat’s mouth was watering again for the treat.
sentences.n: „der Katze wässerte das Maul“ — a dative-of-possession construction (‘to the cat the mouth watered’), i.e. her mouth watered; das Maul is an animal’s mouth. Leckerwerk = dainties, a delicacy (from lecker, ‘tasty’). This opens the third and last episode.

sentences.t: „Aller guten Dinge sind drei,“ sprach sie zu der Maus, „da soll ich wieder Gevatter stehen, das Kind ist ganz schwarz und hat bloß weiße Pfoten, sonst kein weißes Haar am ganzen Leib, das trifft sich alle paar Jahr nur einmal: du lässest mich doch ausgehen?“
sentences.l: „All good things are three,“ she said to the mouse, „there I am to stand godparent again; the child is quite black and has only white paws, otherwise not a white hair on its whole body; that happens only once every few years: you will let me go out, won’t you?“
sentences.i: “All good things come in threes,” she said to the mouse. “I’m to stand godmother yet again — the child is jet-black with only white paws, and not another white hair on its whole body; that only comes about once in a few years. You’ll let me go out, surely?”
sentences.n: „Aller guten Dinge sind drei“ is a proverb, ‘good things come in threes’ — the cat blandly invoking a saying to excuse a third absence. „das trifft sich“ (sich treffen) = ‘it happens, it falls out.’ „du lässest mich doch ausgehen?“ — the particle doch presses gently for a yes.

sentences.t: „Hautab! Halbaus!“ antwortete die Maus, „es sind so kuriose Namen, die machen mich so nachdenksam.“
sentences.l: „Skin-off! Half-gone!“ answered the mouse, „they are such curious names, they make me so pensive.“
sentences.i: “Skin-off! Half-gone!” answered the mouse; “they are such queer names — they set me pondering.”
sentences.n: The mouse turns the two names over uneasily, sensing something amiss without grasping it — dramatic irony, since the ‘names’ are a running account of the theft. kuriose = curious, odd; nachdenksam = pensive, given to brooding.

sentences.t: „Da sitzest du daheim in deinem dunkelgrauen Flausrock und deinem langen Haarzopf,“ sprach die Katze, „und fängst Grillen: das kommt davon wenn man bei Tage nicht ausgeht.“
sentences.l: „There you sit at home in your dark-grey frieze-coat and your long hair-braid,“ said the cat, „and catch crickets: that comes of when one does not go out by day.“
sentences.i: “There you sit at home in your dark-grey frieze coat and your long pigtail,” said the cat, “brooding away — that’s what comes of never going out by day.”
sentences.n: The cat mockingly dresses up the mouse: Flausrock, a coat of shaggy frieze (Flaus, thick woollen cloth), and Haarzopf, a plait — the mouse’s grey fur and thin tail. „Grillen fangen“ (‘to catch crickets’) is an idiom for brooding, giving way to gloomy fancies.

sentences.t: Die Maus räumte während der Abwesenheit der Katze auf und brachte das Haus in Ordnung, die naschhafte Katze aber fraß den Fetttopf rein aus.
sentences.l: The mouse tidied up during the cat’s absence and brought the house into order; the nibble-loving cat, however, ate the fat-pot clean out.
sentences.i: The mouse tidied up while the cat was away and put the house in order; but the greedy cat ate the pot of fat completely empty.
sentences.n: „räumte … auf“ is the separable aufräumen (‘to tidy up’). naschhaft = fond of sweets, given to nibbling treats (from naschen). „fraß … rein aus“ — rein (‘clean, entirely’) intensifies ausfressen: she emptied it utterly, setting up the third name, Ganzaus.

sentences.t: „Wenn erst alles aufgezehrt ist, so hat man Ruhe“ sagte sie zu sich selbst und kam satt und dick erst in der Nacht nach Haus.
sentences.l: „When once everything is eaten up, then one has peace,“ she said to herself, and came home, sated and fat, only in the night.
sentences.i: “Once it’s all eaten up, then a body has peace,” she said to herself, and came home, full and fat, not until the dead of night.
sentences.n: „aufgezehrt“ (aufzehren, to consume utterly) — the past participle. „satt und dick“ = full and fat. „erst in der Nacht“ = not until nightfall (erst marking the lateness).

sentences.t: Die Maus fragte gleich nach dem Namen, den das dritte Kind bekommen hätte.
sentences.l: The mouse asked at once after the name that the third child had gotten.
sentences.i: The mouse asked at once what name the third child had been given.
sentences.n: „bekommen hätte“ — subjunctive II in an indirect question, marking the name as reported rather than the narrator’s own assertion; this reported-speech subjunctive runs all through Grimm.

sentences.t: „Er wird dir wohl auch nicht gefallen,“ sagte die Katze, „er heißt Ganzaus.“
sentences.l: „It will probably not please you either,“ said the cat, „it is called All-gone.“
sentences.i: “You probably won’t care for this one either,” said the cat; “its name is All-gone.”
sentences.n: „er wird dir … gefallen“ — gefallen takes the dative person (‘to please someone’); er/der refers back to Namen. „Ganzaus,“ from „ganz aus“ (‘all out/gone’), is the third and last invented name: the pot is now entirely empty.

sentences.t: „Ganzaus!“ rief die Maus, „das ist der allerbedenklichste Namen, gedruckt ist er mir noch nicht vorgekommen.
sentences.l: „All-gone!“ cried the mouse, „that is the most alarming name of all; in print it has not yet come before me.
sentences.i: “All-gone!” cried the mouse. “That’s the most disturbing name yet — I’ve never once come across it in print.
sentences.n: allerbedenklichste is the superlative of bedenklich (‘worrying, dubious’) with the intensifier aller- (‘of all’). „gedruckt ist er mir noch nicht vorgekommen“ — vorkommen (to occur, turn up) with dative mir: ‘it has never come my way in print.’

sentences.t: Ganzaus! was soll das bedeuten?“
sentences.l: All-gone! what is that supposed to mean?“
sentences.i: All-gone! What is that supposed to mean?”
sentences.n: „was soll das bedeuten?“ = ‘what is that meant to mean?’ — the mouse is one small step from the truth, yet never takes it.

sentences.t: Sie schüttelte den Kopf, rollte sich zusammen und legte sich schlafen.
sentences.l: She shook her head, rolled herself together, and lay herself down to sleep.
sentences.i: She shook her head, curled herself up, and lay down to sleep.
sentences.n: „rollte sich zusammen“ (sich zusammenrollen) = to curl up. „legte sich schlafen“ = lay down to sleep — the reflexive with a bare infinitive of purpose.

sentences.t: Von nun an wollte niemand mehr die Katze zu Gevatter bitten, als aber der Winter herangekommen und draußen nichts mehr zu finden war, gedachte die Maus ihres Vorraths und sprach „komm Katze, wir wollen zu unserm Fetttopfe gehen, den wir uns aufgespart haben, der wird uns schmecken.“
sentences.l: From now on no one wanted to ask the cat to be godparent any more; but when the winter had come on and outside there was nothing more to find, the mouse bethought herself of her store and said, „come, cat, we will go to our fat-pot that we have saved up for ourselves; it will taste good to us.“
sentences.i: From then on no one cared to ask the cat to stand godmother any longer; but when winter had come and there was nothing more to be found out of doors, the mouse remembered her store and said, “Come, cat, let’s go to our pot of fat that we saved up — it will be a treat.”
sentences.n: herangekommen (herankommen, ‘to come on, approach’) — of winter setting in. „gedachte … ihres Vorraths“ — gedenken (‘to be mindful of, remember’) takes the genitive (ihres Vorraths); Vorrath keeps the old -th- (modern Vorrat). „aufgespart“ (aufsparen) = saved up, laid by.

sentences.t: „Ja wohl,“ antwortete die Katze, „der wird dir schmecken als wenn du deine feine Zunge zum Fenster hinaus streckst.“
sentences.l: „Yes indeed,“ answered the cat, „it will taste to you as if you stuck your fine tongue out of the window.“
sentences.i: “Oh yes,” answered the cat, “it’ll taste about as good as sticking your dainty tongue out of the window.”
sentences.n: „der wird dir schmecken als wenn …“ — heavy irony: since the pot is empty, the feast will be as satisfying as poking your tongue out into the cold air. streckst is 2nd sg. of strecken (‘to stretch, stick out’).

sentences.t: Sie machten sich auf den Weg, und als sie anlangten, stand zwar der Fetttopf noch an seinem Platz, er war aber leer.
sentences.l: They set out on their way, and when they arrived, the fat-pot indeed still stood in its place, but it was empty.
sentences.i: They set out, and when they got there the pot of fat did still stand in its place — but it was empty.
sentences.n: „machten sich auf den Weg“ = to set out (a fixed reflexive idiom). „zwar … aber …“ = ‘indeed … but …,’ conceding the pot was there before delivering the blow that it was empty. anlangten (anlangen) = arrived.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sagte die Maus, „jetzt merke ich was geschehen ist, jetzt kommts an den Tag, du bist mir die wahre Freundin! aufgefressen hast du alles, wie du zu Gevatter gestanden hast: erst Haut ab, dann halb aus, dann …“
sentences.l: „Ah,“ said the mouse, „now I notice what has happened, now it comes to light: you are a true friend to me indeed! eaten up you have everything, while you stood as godparent: first skin off, then half out, then …“
sentences.i: “Ah,” said the mouse, “now I see what has happened — now it all comes out! A fine friend you’ve been to me! You ate up the whole lot while you were supposedly standing godmother: first Skin-off, then Half-gone, then —”
sentences.n: The mouse finally reads the three names for what they are, splitting them back into their plain words: „Haut ab“ (Hautab), „halb aus“ (Halbaus), and — cut off before she can finish — „ganz aus“ (Ganzaus). „kommts an den Tag“ (= kommt es; „an den Tag kommen“ = to come to light). „du bist mir die wahre Freundin“ is bitterly ironic — ‘a fine friend you are.’ aufgefressen (auffressen) = eaten up, devoured.

sentences.t: „Willst du schweigen“ rief die Katze, „noch ein Wort, und ich fresse dich auf.“
sentences.l: „Will you be silent,“ cried the cat, „one more word, and I eat you up.“
sentences.i: “Will you hold your tongue!” cried the cat. “One more word and I’ll eat you up.”
sentences.n: „Willst du schweigen“ — a threat framed as a question, ‘will you be quiet!’ „fresse dich auf“ — auffressen once more, the animal’s devouring; the threat is meant literally, as the next line proves.

sentences.t: „Ganz aus“ hatte die arme Maus schon auf der Zunge, kaum war es heraus, so that die Katze einen Satz nach ihr, packte sie und schluckte sie hinunter.
sentences.l: „All out“ the poor mouse already had on her tongue; scarcely was it out, so the cat made a leap at her, seized her, and swallowed her down.
sentences.i: “All-gone” was already on the poor mouse’s tongue; scarcely was it out when the cat sprang at her, seized her, and gulped her down.
sentences.n: „Ganz aus“ — the mouse says aloud the plain words behind the third name, the very instant of understanding and of her death. „auf der Zunge haben“ = to have on the tip of one’s tongue. that is the 1857 spelling of tat (thun/tun); „einen Satz thun“ = to make a leap (Satz here = a bound, spring). „schluckte … hinunter“ (hinunterschlucken) = swallowed down.

sentences.t: Siehst du, so gehts in der Welt.
sentences.l: You see, so it goes in the world.
sentences.i: You see — that’s the way of the world.
sentences.n: The narrator’s dry closing shrug. „so gehts in der Welt“ (= geht es) = ‘that’s how it goes in the world’ — the fable’s bleak moral, with no comfort offered.
